title: New moon, full moon: two monthly anchors
subtitle: A ritual for using the lunar cycle as a framework for intention and release
excerpt: A ritual for using new moon and full moon, as monthly anchors. One for setting intention, one for releasing what isn't needed. No spiritual belief required, only a willingness to mark time deliberately.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The lunar cycle as a structural anchor is a cross-cultural practice; this version is secular.

practice.whenToUse: Twice a month, at the new moon and full moon — or whenever you want to build a regular rhythm of intentional reflection into the calendar

content.text: A ritual for using the lunar cycle as a structural framework: a reliable calendar marker for two types of intentional pause. No spiritual belief required. The new moon for beginning. The full moon for releasing.
Paper. A pen. Somewhere quiet for fifteen minutes.

Sit quietly for two minutes before you write anything. Then write one to three intentions for the coming month: directions of attention, not tasks or goals. What do you want to move toward? What quality do you want to bring? Keep them short. Write them somewhere you'll see them.
Paper. A pen. The intentions from the new moon if you kept them.

Look back at the new moon intentions if you have them. What moved? What didn't? Then write one to three things you are releasing: a belief, a resentment, a habit, an old story about yourself. Write them down, then fold the paper and put it somewhere you won't keep reading it. The release is the act of writing it and putting it down.
By homemade.education. The lunar cycle as a marker appears across many cultural traditions. This version is secular.
